title: Accounts Receivable & Billing
subhead: Revenue recognition under ASC 606, the invoicing workflow, the AR aging report, and the allowance method that keeps bad debt honest rather than ignored.
hook: "When do you record revenue on a $2.4 million contract?" The wrong answer is "when we get paid" or "when the contract is signed." The right answer walks through the five-step ASC 606 model and names exactly when each deliverable is satisfied. After this module, that is your answer.

chunks.title: Revenue Recognition in 3 Minutes (ASC 606)
chunks.intro: A client signs a $2.4M contract. When do you record the $2.4M? The wrong answer costs companies millions in restatements.
chunks.transcript:
0:00 — A client signs a $2.4 million contract. When do you record the $2.4M of revenue? The wrong answer costs companies millions in restatements, so listen carefully.

0:20 — ASC 606 is a five-step model. Identify the contract. Identify the distinct performance obligations — for a fiber design that's HLD, LLD, and a splice/permit package, three separate obligations. Determine the transaction price. Allocate that price to each obligation. Then recognize revenue when (or as) each obligation is satisfied.

1:00 — For Skarion's $2.4M contract over 3,000 miles: the $2.4M is not revenue at signing. It's not revenue when cash arrives. It becomes revenue as each deliverable is accepted — 40% at HLD delivery, 40% at LLD, 20% at the final permit package.

1:40 — Two balance-sheet accounts keep this honest. Deferred Revenue is cash received before work is done — a liability, not revenue. Unbilled AR (WIP) is work done but not yet at a billing milestone — an asset, value already created. Both exist precisely because "when cash moves" is the wrong question.

2:20 — Get this wrong and you overstate revenue. Get it right and your financials match the economics of the contract. That's the entire point of ASC 606.

chunks.title: Revenue recognition and ASC 606 — the 5-step model
chunks.bodyHtml:
ASC 606 is the GAAP standard for revenue recognition, and it is built on one principle: recognize revenue when (or as) the entity satisfies a performance obligation — not when cash arrives, and not when the contract is signed. The model has five steps:

  1. Identify the contract with the customer.
  2. Identify the distinct performance obligations — e.g. HLD delivery, LLD delivery, splice/permit package are three separate obligations.
  3. Determine the transaction price.
  4. Allocate the transaction price to each performance obligation.
  5. Recognize revenue when (or as) each obligation is satisfied.

Why it matters at Skarion: the firm signs a $2.4M contract for a 3,000-mile fiber design. The $2.4M is not revenue when the contract is signed, and it is not revenue when the cash arrives. It becomes revenue only as each deliverable is completed and accepted. Getting this wrong overstates revenue and triggers audit restatements.

Two accounts keep this honest on the balance sheet:

  • Deferred Revenue (a liability) — client pays before work is done. The cash is in, but it is not yet earned.
  • Unbilled AR / WIP (an asset) — work is done or in progress but the billing milestone has not triggered yet. Value has been created; the invoice just has not been cut.

chunks.title: When the $2.4M becomes revenue — milestone timeline

steps.title: Contract signed
steps.desc: Skarion signs a $2.4M design contract for 3,000 miles. No revenue yet — only a performance obligation is created.

steps.title: HLD delivered (40%)
steps.desc: High-Level Design package accepted by the client. Recognize $960,000 of revenue. Debit AR / Credit Engineering Design Revenue.

steps.title: LLD delivered (40%)
steps.desc: Low-Level Design completion approved. Recognize a further $960,000 of revenue at the LLD milestone.

steps.title: Splice/Permit package (20%)
steps.desc: Final permitting and splice documentation delivered. Recognize the remaining $480,000. Contract fully earned.

chunks.title: Creating the AR invoice and recording it
chunks.bodyHtml: An AR invoice is not generated casually. The workflow is: project milestone triggers the invoice → Project Manager approves → accounting creates the invoice in the billing system → journal entry debits Accounts Receivable and credits Engineering Design Revenue. The invoice then sits open in the AR subledger until cash is applied in Module 05.

callout.html: Internal control signal: invoice numbers are sequential. A gap in the sequence is a red flag an auditor will chase — it suggests a voided, deleted, or skipped invoice. Always be able to explain every invoice number gap with a documented void or credit memo.

chunks.title: Analyze a Skarion AR invoice — what every field controls

hotspots.label: Invoice number
hotspots.info: Sequential. Gaps are an internal control red flag — every gap needs a documented void or credit memo.

hotspots.label: Contract reference number
hotspots.info: Ties the invoice to the master services agreement and the original ASC 606 contract — audit trail.

hotspots.label: Line items by deliverable
hotspots.info: HLD for Section A, B, C broken out — never a single lump sum. This supports the performance-obligation allocation under ASC 606.

hotspots.label: Payment terms
hotspots.info: Net-45 is typical for telecom clients. Drives DSO and when the AR ages into the next bucket.

hotspots.label: Remittance instructions
hotspots.info: Wire instructions and ACH routing — verify these against a call-back confirmation to stop vendor impersonation fraud.

chunks.title: Invoice an HLD milestone and book the revenue
chunks.scenarioHtml: Skarion completes the High-Level Design for the Tennessee Corridor. Per the contract's ASC 606 allocation, this milestone is 40% of the $1.2M contract, so Skarion invoices the client $480,000. No cash yet — record the receivable and the earned revenue.

chunks.title: Client prepays — record the deferred revenue liability
chunks.scenarioHtml: A client prepays $120,000 before any work has begun. Cash is in the bank, but the performance obligation has not been satisfied — so it is a liability, not revenue. Record the cash receipt and the deferred revenue liability.

chunks.title: Deliver the work — release the deferred revenue
chunks.scenarioHtml: The work the client prepaid for is now delivered and accepted. The performance obligation is satisfied — release the $120,000 from Deferred Revenue onto the income statement as earned Engineering Design Revenue.

chunks.title: What is Accounts Receivable Aging and Why It Matters
chunks.intro: A business can be profitable and still run out of cash. The AR aging report tells you why.
chunks.transcript:
0:00 — A business can be profitable and still run out of cash. The AR aging report tells you why.

0:10 — The aging report buckets every open invoice by how long it has gone unpaid: 0–30 days (current, on terms), 31–60, 61–90, and 90+ days (delinquent). As invoices age into the older buckets, the risk of non-collection rises and cash is tied up instead of in the bank.

0:45 — Here is Skarion's aging snapshot. Atlantic Fiber: $480,000, all current. Southeast Broadband: $405,000, half in the 31–60 bucket — watch it. TeleCom Builders: $112,700, split between 61–90 and 90+ days — this is the problem customer, and they get a dunning letter this week.

1:30 — Total AR is $997,700. Against $4.8M annual credit revenue, that's a DSO of about 75.8 days — it takes Skarion 76 days to collect a dollar of billed revenue. Controllers watch DSO month over month as a leading indicator of cash flow trouble.

2:00 — The aging report is how you turn AR from a number on a balance sheet into an action list: who to call, who to escalate, and which balances to add to the allowance for doubtful accounts.

chunks.title: AR aging, collections, and bad debt
chunks.bodyHtml:
The AR Aging Report is the single most important AR management tool. It buckets every customer's outstanding invoices by how long they have been unpaid. As buckets grow to 90+ days, the firm escalates: dunning letters, account-manager outreach, and eventually collections or write-off.

Skarion's AR aging snapshot:

Customer0–30 days31–60 days61–90 days90+ daysTotal
Atlantic Fiber Co.$480,000———$480,000
Southeast Broadband$215,000$190,000——$405,000
TeleCom Builders——$78,500$34,200$112,700
Total$695,000$190,000$78,500$34,200$997,700

TeleCom Builders is the problem customer here: $112,700 sitting entirely in the older buckets, $34,200 of it past 90 days. That calls for a dunning letter and an account-manager call this week.

Allowance for Doubtful Accounts. GAAP requires you to estimate uncollectible AR as you earn revenue — not wait until a specific invoice goes bad. This is the allowance method, and it matches the bad debt expense to the period that produced the revenue (the matching principle).

  • Establish the allowance: Debit Bad Debt Expense / Credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (a contra-asset that reduces net AR).
  • Write off a specific invoice: Debit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts / Credit Accounts Receivable. Expense is NOT affected at write-off — the expense was already recorded when the allowance was established.

DSO (Days Sales Outstanding): Average AR ÷ Credit Revenue × Days. Skarion: $997,700 ÷ $4.8M × 365 ≈ 75.8 days — meaning it takes roughly 76 days to collect a dollar of billed revenue. The lower, the better; controllers watch DSO month-over-month as a leading cash-flow indicator.

chunks.title: AR Aging Worksheet — Skarion (live Excel)
chunks.scenarioHtml: Build Skarion's AR aging analysis directly in the spreadsheet. Fill in the four aging buckets (0–30, 31–60, 61–90, 90+) per customer from the AR subledger snapshot in Module 04. The lab validates when the four bucket columns each total correctly, the grand total equals $997,700, and the sum of all bucket totals equals the total AR. The TeleCom Builders 90+ balance is the red flag — note it.
chunks.successMessage: ✅ Aging balances to $997,700 total. TeleCom Builders' $34,200 in the 90+ bucket is the red flag — dunning letter this week.
chunks.errorMessage: ❌ Bucket totals don't match yet. Totals should be 0–30=$695,000 · 31–60=$190,000 · 61–90=$78,500 · 90+=$34,200 (sum $997,700). Check Atlantic Fiber ($480,000 all current) and TeleCom ($78,500 / $34,200 in the older buckets).

chunks.title: Bad Debt — Estimating What You Won't Collect (Allowance Method)
chunks.intro: GAAP doesn't let you wait until a customer files bankruptcy to record the loss. You estimate it every month.
chunks.transcript:
0:00 — GAAP doesn't let you wait until a customer files bankruptcy to record the loss. You estimate it every month, in the period you actually earned the revenue — that's the matching principle.

0:15 — The allowance method uses two entries. First, establish the allowance: estimate a percentage of AR you won't collect and post Dr Bad Debt Expense / Cr Allowance for Doubtful Accounts — a contra-asset that reduces net AR. Skarion estimates 1.5% of $997,700 = $14,965.50.

0:55 — Second, write off a specific invoice when it's confirmed uncollectible: Dr Allowance for Doubtful Accounts / Cr Accounts Receivable. Notice: Bad Debt Expense is not touched at write-off — the expense was already recorded when the allowance was set up.

1:35 — The surprise recovery. TeleCom Builders unexpectedly pays $40,000 six months after being written off. You first reverse the write-off (Dr AR $40,000 / Cr Allowance $40,000), then record the cash receipt (Dr Cash $40,000 / Cr AR $40,000). The net effect is Dr Cash $40,000 / Cr Allowance $40,000 — recovery flows back through the allowance, not through revenue.

2:15 — Compare this with the direct write-off method, which only records bad debt when a specific invoice fails. It's not GAAP-compliant because it books the expense in the wrong period — but small cash-basis firms sometimes use it. Know the difference; an interview will ask.

chunks.title: AR vocabulary — five terms interviewers actually test

cards.back: Days Sales Outstanding = Average AR ÷ Credit Revenue × Days. Skarion: $997,700 ÷ $4.8M × 365 ≈ 75.8 days. Measures how fast billed revenue converts to cash.

Estimate uncollectible AR as a percentage of revenue and record Bad Debt Expense in the period the sale is made. The allowance is a contra-asset reducing net AR. Required by GAAP — matching principle.

Record bad debt only when a specific invoice is identified as uncollectible. Not GAAP-compliant for accrual filers because it violates the matching principle — expense lands in the wrong period. Used by small cash-basis firms only.

The escalating outreach process for overdue AR — reminder, then formal dunning letter, then account-manager call, then collections or write-off. Skarion's TeleCom Builders $112,700 balance triggers a dunning letter this week.

Selling AR to a third party (a factor) for immediate cash, at a discount. Removes the receivable from the books (if sold without recourse) but is expensive — typically 1–3% of invoice value as the factor's fee.

chunks.title: Record the allowance for doubtful accounts
chunks.scenarioHtml: Based on Skarion's $997,700 AR balance and historical loss rates, management estimates 1.5% will be uncollectible. Establish the allowance by recording Bad Debt Expense and the contra-asset Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (1.5% of $997,700 = $14,965.50).

chunks.title: Write off TeleCom Builders after Chapter 11
chunks.scenarioHtml: TeleCom Builders files Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Their entire $112,700 balance is deemed uncollectible. Write it off against the existing allowance. Note: Bad Debt Expense is not touched at write-off — the expense was already recorded when the allowance was established.

chunks.title: TeleCom Builders pays after write-off — reverse and record cash
chunks.scenarioHtml: Six months later, out of the bankruptcy estate, TeleCom Builders unexpectedly pays $40,000. Under the allowance method you first reverse the write-off (reinstate the receivable), then record the cash receipt. Post both: Dr AR $40,000 / Cr Allowance $40,000, then Dr Cash $40,000 / Cr AR $40,000. Enter it as the combined net effect.

chunks.intro: Four AR and revenue-recognition questions that show up in entry-level and senior interviews alike.
chunks.title: Revenue recognition & AR quiz

questions.q: Under ASC 606, when does Skarion recognize revenue on a contract milestone?
options: When the client signs the contract
When the client wires the payment
When the specific performance obligation (e.g. HLD delivery) is satisfied
questions.explanation: ASC 606 recognizes revenue when (or as) each distinct performance obligation is satisfied — here, when the HLD is delivered and accepted. Not at contract signing, not at cash receipt.

questions.q: A client pays $200,000 in advance for work that has not yet started. What account is credited?
questions.explanation: Cash received before the obligation is satisfied is a liability — Deferred Revenue — not earned revenue. It only becomes revenue when the work is delivered.

questions.q: What is the journal entry to establish an allowance for doubtful accounts?
options: Debit Bad Debt Expense / Credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts
Debit Bad Debt Expense / Credit AR
questions.explanation: Match the bad-debt estimate to the period of the sale: Debit Bad Debt Expense, Credit Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (a contra-asset). AR itself is untouched at this stage.

questions.q: Skarion has $997,700 average AR and $4.8M in annual credit revenue. What is the DSO?
questions.explanation: DSO = Average AR ÷ Credit Revenue × 365 = $997,700 ÷ $4,800,000 × 365 ≈ 75.8 days. It takes Skarion roughly 76 days to collect a dollar of billed revenue.

chunks.title: Scenario Lab — The Client Who Pays in Three Pieces
chunks.intro: A customer sends three separate wires over six weeks, one without remittance, one with a surprise early-pay discount. Apply each correctly and decide what to do with the remaining short payment.
chunks.setup:
Southeast Broadband owes Skarion $405,000 on two open invoices: SE-2026-004 ($215,000) and SE-2026-005 ($190,000). Their agreed terms are 2/10 Net 30. They send three separate wires over six weeks:

  • Wire 1 — $215,000 with a note: "Payment for Invoice SE-2026-004"
  • Wire 2 — $100,000 with no remittance advice and no reference number
  • Wire 3 — $89,750 with a note: "Final payment less $250 wire processing fee"
tasks: Wire 1: identify the invoice, confirm the payment clears SE-2026-004 exactly, and post Dr Cash $215,000 / Cr AR $215,000.

Wire 2: no remittance advice — before posting anything, confirm with Southeast Broadband which invoice it applies to and document the chase. Assume it applies to SE-2026-005 (the older open invoice) only after written confirmation.

Wire 3: confirm the short-pay reason. Wire 3 is 28 days after SE-2026-005's invoice date (inside the Net 30 window but after any early-pay discount period under 2/10 Net 30). State whether the $250 short-pay (a claimed wire processing fee) is valid under the terms, and how to handle it.
Reconcile all three: total paid vs. total owed. Note the remaining short payment and open a dispute with a documented reason (no valid early-pay discount was earned within the 10-day window).
Post the journal entries for all three wires, including any discount taken as a contra-revenue or expense as the policy dictates, and the documented short-pay balance remaining in AR.
chunks.submissionPrompt: Paste your three journal entries, your remittance-advice chase note for Wire 2, your discount-validity conclusion for Wire 3, and the remaining AR short-pay with the dispute documentation you'd file.
chunks.solution:
Wire 1 ($215,000, SE-2026-004): Dr 1000 Operating Checking $215,000 / Cr 1100 Accounts Receivable $215,000. SE-2026-004 is fully cleared.

Wire 2 ($100,000, no remittance): Do NOT post blind. Email the customer: "We received a $100,000 wire on [date] with no reference — please confirm which invoice it applies to." After written confirmation it applies to SE-2026-005: Dr 1000 Cash $100,000 / Cr 1100 AR $100,000 (partial payment against SE-2026-005; remaining $90,000 stays open).

Wire 3 ($89,750, SE-2026-005 remainder): Terms are 2/10 Net 30 — the 2% discount is only valid if paid within 10 days of invoice. At 28 days the early-pay discount is not valid, so no discount should be deducted. The $250 short-pay is the customer's claimed wire processing fee, not a contractually earned discount. The full $90,000 remains owed on SE-2026-005; $89,750 was received, so $250 is a short payment.

Short-pay = $250 (claimed wire fee, not a valid discount). Post the cash received and document the dispute; do not book a discount: Dr 1000 Cash $89,750 / Cr 1100 AR $89,750. The remaining $250 stays open in the AR subledger with a dispute flag — follow up in writing and request the $250 remittance. A wire fee is the customer's cost unless your contract specifically allows them to pass it on; never silently write off a disputed short payment without manager approval.

Total reconciled: $215,000 + $100,000 + $89,750 = $404,750 collected of $405,000 owed; $250 disputed short-pay remains open and documented.
